identity.title: Photographic Film, Paper, Plate, Chemical, and Copy Toner Manufacturing

lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers of photographic film, photographic paper and plates, photographic chemicals, and copy toner that sell repeat consumables to external business or consumer-channel customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive production, pure distributors and resellers, printer or camera manufacturing, photo laboratories, software-only offerings, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeated product sales under distributor, dealer, enterprise, laboratory, studio, and commercial-printer relationships; revenue is generally per unit or under supply arrangements rather than hourly service billing.

a.rationale: Core coating, chemical formulation, converting, packaging, maintenance, and laboratory-quality work is physical and process constrained. AI is more applicable to production scheduling, yield analysis, predictive maintenance, visual inspection triage, documentation, purchasing, sales support, and administration. The range is a wage-weighted judgment rather than a direct industry measurement.
a.change_evidence: A 325992-specific OEWS staffing matrix combined with plant-level time studies separating manual production, quality, maintenance, engineering, sales, and administrative hours and documenting current automation.
caveats: No six-digit occupation-by-task AI exposure study was located.
The code combines technically different film, plate, chemical, and toner processes.

rho.rationale: Over five years, mature analytics, document copilots, planning tools, and machine-vision assistance can penetrate support and selected quality workflows, but validated recipes, safety controls, equipment integration, scarce process data, and customer qualification limit implementation of the exposed opportunity.
rho.change_evidence: Structured interviews with plant managers and quality leaders at independent US film, plate, chemical, and toner plants, plus vendor deployment records showing labor hours removed after twelve months.
caveats: Implementation evidence is inferred from occupational responsibilities rather than observed deployments in 325992.
Large incumbents may have more capital and data than LMM firms.

e.rationale: Repeat consumables support recurring customer relationships, but eligibility is reduced by concentrated proprietary production, multinational or captive operations, distributor-only businesses, customer concentration, environmental liabilities, and facilities whose economics depend on scale or specialized coating assets.
e.change_evidence: A complete establishment-to-firm census for 325992 with ownership, EBITDA, customer concentration, recurring-sales mix, environmental liabilities, and whether production IP and permits transfer with control.
caveats: The injected firm count is an estimate and may misclassify diversified manufacturers.
Kodak illustrates concentration and customer dependence but is far larger than the lens.

s5.bridge: Census ABS owner-age tables measure employer-business owner demographics across sectors, not qualifying control transfers among 325992 LMM manufacturers. The estimate translates broad succession exposure into a five-year transfer probability, then discounts for family/internal succession, non-sale closures, and nontransferable environmental or technical operations. Sector-wide owner data may overstate this code if its firms are more often corporate subsidiaries, or understate it if niche plants have unusually old founders.
s5.rationale: Broad employer-business succession exposure makes some control turnover plausible, but this niche includes corporate divisions and technically concentrated plants that do not map cleanly to ordinary owner-retirement statistics. A wide range reflects the absence of code-specific closed-deal counts.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, and broker records tagged to 325992, reconciled to an establishment roster and classified by true control transfer, EBITDA band, and operating continuity.
caveats: ABS is not industry-specific deal-flow evidence.
Owner age does not establish willingness or ability to sell a going concern.

q.rationale: Unit-priced specialty consumables allow an operator to keep a meaningful share of cost savings until contract renewal or competitive repricing. Qualification costs, formulation consistency, and constrained supply can slow pass-through, while powerful distributors, print OEMs, laboratories, and concentrated accounts can demand concessions. Kodak's reported gross-profit benefit from pricing shows pricing can matter, but does not isolate retention of AI benefits.
q.change_evidence: Customer-contract review covering cost-plus clauses, indexation, rebates, renewal cadence, most-favored-customer terms, and realized price/cost bridges before and after documented process improvements.
caveats: No source directly measures five-year retention of implemented AI benefits.
Bargaining power differs sharply between niche film, commercial plates, chemicals, and commodity-compatible toner.

d5.bridge: Kodak segment and line-of-business disclosures capture one large diversified supplier rather than total constant-quality US 325992 quantity. Reported weak plate demand and digital substitution imply downside, while growth in film and advanced-materials volume provides an offset. Revenue signals are converted judgmentally to physical demand and may overstate quantity when price increases drive sales or understate niche-film strength masked by declining print plates.
d5.rationale: Commercial print plates and office toner face continuing digital substitution, but motion-picture and enthusiast film, specialized imaging consumables, and chemical applications retain niches. The mixed product basket supports a modest base decline with substantial dispersion.
d5.change_evidence: US shipment volumes by product class from Census Current Industrial Reports or a trade association, decomposed into film, plates, photographic chemicals, and toner and adjusted for product-mix and quality changes.
caveats: Public company revenue is not a physical quantity index.
The five-year outcome depends heavily on the current revenue mix of the eligible firm.

o.rationale: Demand remaining for film, plates, chemicals, or toner still requires accountable physical manufacture, formulation control, coating or blending, quality release, and supply assurance. Software can reduce end-use printing or photography and automate support, but cannot itself supply the physical consumable represented by remaining year-five demand.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews and product-level churn data separating demand lost to digital self-service from demand switching among physical suppliers, plus evidence of fully autonomous or on-site customer production.
caveats: This is a judgment about the surviving service basket, not a forecast of total demand.
Some compatible toner may shift to offshore or vertically integrated supply rather than disappear.

narrative.executive_view: This is a heterogeneous niche-consumables manufacturing code. AI can improve planning, quality triage, maintenance, documentation, and commercial administration, but most labor remains tied to physical chemical and coating processes. Repeat product demand can support relationships, yet digital substitution and concentrated proprietary capacity create substantial firm-specific risk.
narrative.ai_impact: The practical five-year opportunity is decision support around the plant: schedule and inventory optimization, anomaly detection, predictive maintenance, machine-vision assistance, batch-document drafting, technical knowledge retrieval, and sales-service automation. Recipe control, hazardous-material handling, coating, converting, packing, maintenance, and accountable quality release remain operator-intensive.
narrative.value_capture: Savings inside a fixed or unit-priced consumables contract can initially accrue to the manufacturer. Retention weakens at renewals when sophisticated OEM, distributor, laboratory, or commercial-print customers can benchmark prices, although qualification friction and differentiated formulations can protect some benefit.
narrative.firm_availability: The supplied count is only a starting point. A meaningful portion may be corporate divisions, diversified chemical businesses, captive units, or plants with scale, IP, permitting, contamination, or customer-concentration issues. Genuine transferable independent firms are therefore likely fewer than nominal firms.
narrative.demand_durability: The basket is split: print plates and office toner face electronic-document and inkjet substitution, while motion-picture, enthusiast still film, and specialized chemicals can remain resilient niches. Remaining demand requires physical production and quality accountability, but aggregate quantity is unlikely to be uniformly strong.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The central uncertainties are product mix, concentration, environmental liabilities, technical key-person dependence, customer qualification, and lack of code-specific transfer evidence. Public-company disclosures are imperfect proxies for LMM firms, and price-driven revenue can obscure physical volume.
narrative.principal_driver: Repeat demand for technically qualified physical consumables can preserve customer relationships and allow operational savings to be retained for a time.
narrative.principal_weakness: Digital substitution and concentrated, capital-intensive proprietary production make both demand and the pool of transferable LMM firms uncertain.

sources.title: QCEW Industry Codes and Titles (For NAICS Coded Data)
sources.supports: Confirms the official 325992 industry title and scope label.

sources.title: Industrial Production Managers
sources.supports: Describes plant oversight, worker safety, production operations, and quality-control responsibilities relevant to the task split.

sources.title: Industrial Engineering Technologists and Technicians
sources.supports: Describes workflow, time-and-motion, inventory-cost, quality-assurance, and project-control work that can be assisted by analytics and AI.

sources.title: Eastman Kodak Company 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Reports the composition of Advanced Materials and Chemicals, concentration in Kodak Alaris, weak plate demand, digital substitution, pricing effects, and changing film and chemical volumes.

sources.title: ABS Characteristics of Business Owners: 2022 Tables
sources.supports: Provides employer-business owner age tables used only as a broad succession proxy.

sources.title: Kodak Reports Fourth-Quarter and Full-Year 2025 Financial Results
sources.supports: Reports 2025 segment financial performance and pricing and volume contributions relevant to demand and value-capture judgments.
